skill.name: accessibility-auditor
skill.description: Guides web accessibility audits for WCAG 2.1, ARIA, semantic HTML, keyboard navigation, and assistive technology testing.
skill.summary: Audit web interfaces for WCAG, ARIA, keyboard, form, and screen reader accessibility issues.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, cryptography, sensitive file, and network patterns in SKILL.md. Review found these are Markdown examples, inline accessibility terms, and public W3C resource links, not executable behavior or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fence Markers
low_findings.description: The external command alerts point to Markdown fenced examples and inline tag references. SKILL.md is documentation and contains no Ruby, shell execution, or runnable command wrapper.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is inside Markdown fences or inline Markdown backticks. No executable script file or command invocation exists in the scanned skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keywords
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts match accessibility text such as WCAG levels, ARIA attributes, and descriptions. No hashing, encryption, certificate handling, or crypto library use appears.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows accessibility documentation and ARIA examples rather than cryptographic operations. There is no code path that selects or uses weak algorithms.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Sensitive Key File Pattern
low_findings.description: The sensitive file alerts map to JavaScript event.key examples used for keyboard accessibility. They do not reference private keys, certificates, credential files, or filesystem access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact context is keyboard event handling documentation. The word key is not used as a credential or file reference.

low_findings.title: Benign External Reference Links
low_findings.description: The network alerts identify public W3C accessibility guideline links in a resources section. They are static references and do not perform network requests or transmit data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to public standards documentation and appear as Markdown references. No fetch, curl, webhook, or data exfiltration behavior is present.

content.user_title: Audit Web Accessibility and WCAG Compliance
content.value_statement: Accessibility gaps block users and create compliance risk. This skill helps review interfaces against WCAG, ARIA, keyboard, and screen reader expectations.
actual_capabilities: Explains WCAG 2.1 principles and Level AA or AAA contrast targets.
Reviews common issues with images, forms, headings, landmarks, and buttons.
Gives ARIA guidance for landmarks, live regions, dialogs, accordions, and tabs.
Provides manual testing steps for keyboard, screen reader, zoom, and reflow checks.
Drafts accessibility statement content with conformance status and feedback fields.
limitations: Does not run automated scanners such as axe, WAVE, or Lighthouse by itself.
Does not certify legal compliance with ADA, Section 508, or other laws.
Focuses on WCAG 2.1 guidance, not a complete WCAG 2.2 audit.
Requires real page content or code for project-specific findings.

use_cases.title: Pre-release accessibility review
use_cases.description: Check a new page for missing labels, weak contrast, keyboard gaps, and landmark issues before launch.

use_cases.title: Design system component audit
use_cases.description: Review modal, tab, accordion, button, and form patterns for semantic HTML and ARIA behavior.

use_cases.title: Compliance preparation checklist
use_cases.description: Create a practical WCAG 2.1 testing plan before an ADA or Section 508 review.

prompt_templates.title: Review one page
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner audit of a page or component.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this page for WCAG 2.1 AA issues. Focus on headings, alt text, form labels, contrast, and keyboard access.

prompt_templates.title: Fix form accessibility
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve a form with labels and validation messages.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this form for accessibility. Recommend label, fieldset, error message, and aria-describedby improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Test an interactive component
prompt_templates.scenario: Check a modal, tabs, accordion, or custom control.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this component for keyboard support, focus management, semantic roles, ARIA state, and screen reader behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Create an audit plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan a structured accessibility audit across a product.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a WCAG 2.1 AA audit plan for this product. Include automated checks, manual tests, screen reader tests, and reporting steps.

output_examples.input: Audit a checkout form for accessibility.
output: Findings grouped by labels, validation messages, focus order, and contrast.
Recommended fixes for required fields, error announcements, and grouped payment fields.
Manual tests for keyboard completion and screen reader confirmation.
Checks for dialog role, accessible name, focus trap, Escape handling, and focus restoration.
Notes when native elements should replace custom controls.
A verification checklist for keyboard and screen reader testing.
A plain-language statement with conformance status, feedback contact, known issues, and update date.
Guidance to keep claims accurate and avoid unsupported certification language.

best_practices: Provide the actual page, component markup, or design details being reviewed.
Ask for findings grouped by WCAG principle, severity, and affected user impact.
Combine automated results with manual keyboard and screen reader testing.
anti_patterns: Do not treat automated scanner output as a complete accessibility audit.
Do not add ARIA when native HTML provides the correct semantics.
Do not claim legal compliance without qualified legal and accessibility review.

faq.question: Can this skill certify WCAG compliance?
faq.answer: No. It provides audit guidance and fixes, but certification requires formal testing and qualified review.

faq.question: Does it run accessibility tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It lists tools and testing steps, but it does not execute scanners by itself.

faq.question: Which standard does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on WCAG 2.1, especially Level AA and AAA guidance mentioned in the skill.

faq.question: Can it help with ARIA patterns?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers landmarks, live regions, dialogs, accordions, tabs, and common ARIA states.

faq.question: Is it useful for designers?
faq.answer: Yes. Designers can use it to check contrast, focus behavior, labels, and inclusive interaction patterns.

faq.question: What input gives the best results?
faq.answer: Provide page goals, HTML or component details, known issues, and target compliance level.
